title: NEXT_PUBLIC_* env vars are baked in at build time, not read at runtime
problem: Wondered whether to update the Kubernetes deployment manifest when adding a new NEXT_PUBLIC_ variable. Setting it on the running Pod had no effect.
cause: NEXT_PUBLIC_ is a Next.js compile-time substitution — the value is inlined into the JS bundle during `next build`. The runtime Pod just serves the prebuilt image; there is nothing to inject.
fix: Set NEXT_PUBLIC_* values in the build environment (the host or CI runner that runs `next build`), not the runtime deployment manifest.
prevention: Treat every NEXT_PUBLIC_ variable as a build input. In CI, wire ARG/ENV for each one in the Dockerfile before `RUN next build`. If a value is wrong the bundle silently falls through to any code-side fallback — there is no runtime error.
artifact: Dockerfile build stage; CI build environment

title: A Go struct with no json tags serializes as PascalCase and breaks snake_case clients
problem: A settings page always showed default values regardless of what was saved. The PUT endpoint worked and the DB rows were correct, but the UI never reflected saved values.
cause: The model had gorm column tags but no json tags. Go's encoding/json defaults to the literal Go field name (PascalCase), so the API returned AutoAcceptDelaySeconds while the client read auto_accept_delay_seconds and fell through to defaults on every read.
fix: Added explicit snake_case json tags to every field of the struct. Verified end-to-end with curl that the response used snake_case keys.
prevention: Any Go struct that crosses an HTTP boundary needs explicit json tags from the start. If a struct is both a DB row and an HTTP payload it needs both tag sets: gorm:"column:foo" json:"foo".
artifact: Go model struct used as a JSON response body

title: Partial PUTs clobber non-pointer Go bools to false
problem: Saving any subset of a settings form silently flipped unrelated boolean flags to false. A user toggled a setting on, got a success toast, navigated away, came back — and the value had reverted.
cause: Go's encoding/json maps a missing key to the zero value — false for bool. When the client sends a partial payload, every non-pointer bool on the request struct becomes false, and an unconditional write (row.Flag = req.Flag) clobbers the stored value.
fix: Changed the affected fields from bool to *bool; the handler nil-checks before writing, so an omitted field is left untouched.
prevention: Any field on a partial-update request struct that the caller might omit must be a pointer (*bool, *string, ...), and the handler must nil-check before writing. A client that hardcodes {flag: true} is also a smell — cross-check the field name against the backend DTO.
artifact: Partial-update request DTO and its handler

title: Tailwind v4's oxide native binding fails on Node 18 in CI
problem: A CI build failed with 'Cannot find native binding' pointing at @tailwindcss/oxide, even though the lockfile contained every platform variant.
cause: Tailwind v4 / @tailwindcss/oxide is built against the Node 20+ ABI. On Node 18 the native binding is unloadable regardless of the platform tarballs being present. The error references a platform binding, which misleadingly suggests an install-time architecture problem rather than a Node version one.
fix: Bumped the CI runner's node-version from 18 to 20.
prevention: Pin node-version to 20+ across every workflow in a repo using Tailwind v4. When you see an oxide native-binding error in CI, check the runner's Node major version first, before chasing lockfile contents or architecture flags.

title: GORM `default:true` silently overrides an explicit `false` on Create
problem: Test code seeding a row with Enabled:false produced a row with Enabled=true in the database.
cause: GORM substitutes the column default whenever a Create struct has a field at its Go zero value. For a bool, false IS the zero value — GORM cannot tell 'meant false' from 'untouched', so default:true wins.
fix: Dropped the default:true tag on bool columns whose callers always set the value explicitly. Alternative: make the field *bool so zero-value disambiguation works.
prevention: For config / feature-flag tables whose rows are explicitly seeded, never put default:<bool> in the gorm tag. Reserve gorm defaults for columns where an absent value genuinely means 'use the default' (e.g. created_at).
artifact: GORM model with a bool config column

title: A Postgres jsonb column rejects an empty string — serialize empty as '{}' or NULL
problem: Every INSERT touching a jsonb column failed in production with 'invalid input syntax for type json', on any code path where a helper wrote an empty string for absent structured data. Local SQLite-backed tests did not catch it — SQLite accepts the empty string — so CI was green while production crashed.
cause: Postgres jsonb is strict: an empty string is not valid JSON. The Go zero value for a string field is the empty string, and an obvious-looking helper returned it for the no-data case. Each behaviour is individually correct; the trap is at the seam, and SQLite-backed tests do not enforce it.
fix: Made the helper return the literal {} (valid JSON) for the empty/nil case instead of an empty string. Pinned it with a unit test asserting json.Valid() on the empty input.
prevention: When introducing a jsonb column backed by a Go string: either return a valid JSON literal for the empty case, or switch the field to *string and return nil to write SQL NULL. Mixing jsonb columns with SQLite test infra is a known false-green pattern — add at least one Postgres-backed test for the empty path.

title: Don't conflate two state machines into one JSON response field
problem: A response field tagged order_status was populated with the DELIVERY status, not the ORDER status — two independent lifecycles packed into one string. Clients gated their UI on it as if it were the order status. For most traffic the mismatched value never matched a client branch by accident, so it looked fine. An edge case eventually emptied the field, a defensive client-side fallback turned it into a valid-looking state, and a button appeared that triggered a 409. The bug ran silently for months.
cause: Two independent state machines — different terminal states, different transition rules — were modeled in one response field. The field's name (order-side) did not match its populating source (delivery-side). Nobody caught it at review because the values never visibly clashed until an edge case emptied one.
fix: Split the field in two — one named field per state machine, each populated from its own source. Dropped the client's defensive fallback so a missing value leaves the state undefined and the UI gate simply skips. Shipped all changes in lockstep.
prevention: One named field = one state machine. If a response must surface two lifecycles, give it two fields, even if the values look interchangeable today. The field name and its populating source must match — reviewing OrderStatus: deliveryStatus should be a red flag. A `value || 'default'` fallback on the client is usually smoke from an ambiguous backend shape.
artifact: API response struct and its client consumer

title: CORS AllowMethods must include PATCH or the browser aborts before the request is sent
problem: A new PATCH endpoint surfaced as a generic 'Network Error' — the request never reached the backend. GET and POST from the same app worked fine. The network tab showed an OPTIONS preflight returning 200, with an Access-Control-Allow-Methods list that did not include PATCH.
cause: PATCH was never enumerated in the backend's CORS AllowMethods — no PATCH endpoint had existed before, so this was the first. Browsers preflight PATCH (it is not a CORS-safe method); the preflight 'succeeds' with 200 but signals PATCH is not allowed, and the browser silently aborts the real request. The HTTP client reports only a generic 'Network Error' because there is no real response.
fix: Added 'PATCH' to the CORS AllowMethods list. One line.
prevention: When adding a new HTTP method to a backend — especially PATCH, the most likely 'first of its kind' in an established codebase — confirm the method is in the CORS AllowMethods list. Tell-tale signs: a 'Network Error' with no status code, an OPTIONS preflight that 200s with no follow-up request, and the endpoint working fine via curl (which never preflights).

title: Deleting a Next.js app-router route leaves a stale generated validator — phantom tsc error
problem: After deleting an app-router route file and every link to it, `tsc -b` failed with TS2307: Cannot find module ...page.js, originating from .next/types/validator.ts. ESLint passed; only the type-check failed.
cause: .next/types/validator.ts is generated by Next.js and enumerates every route. It is only regenerated by `next dev` / `next build`, never by a bare `tsc -b`. The tsconfig include glob pulls in .next/types, so a standalone type-check reads the stale generated file that still imports the deleted route.
fix: rm -rf .next (or .next/types), then re-run the type-check. `next build` later regenerates a correct validator.
prevention: After deleting or renaming any app-router route, clear .next before trusting a standalone tsc type-check — otherwise a phantom TS2307 points at a route that is correctly gone.

title: For 'one active config per scope', use a partial unique index — GORM .First() silently picks the oldest duplicate
problem: An admin-managed config table had no uniqueness on its scope key, so staff could create duplicate active rows for the same scope. Resolution used GORM .First() with no Order(), so the oldest duplicate silently won — while the admin list showed newest-first. A corrected value could read 'Active' in the UI yet never take effect.
cause: No unique constraint on the scope key. GORM .First() orders by primary key, so 'which duplicate applies' resolved to the lowest id = oldest insert — deterministic but invisible, and the opposite of the operator's 'latest wins' expectation.
fix: Added a partial unique index: CREATE UNIQUE INDEX ... (scope_key) WHERE enabled. This permits at most one ENABLED row per scope while still allowing many disabled history rows — a plain unique index would forbid keeping history. Added an app-level pre-check returning a typed error so the API answers 409 instead of a raw constraint error.
prevention: For any config table where exactly one row should be in effect per scope, add a partial unique index WHERE <active-flag> from the start; never rely on .First() ordering to disambiguate. If the live table already has duplicates, CREATE UNIQUE INDEX will fail — dedupe first, and verify the index exists post-deploy rather than trusting a green boot.
artifact: DB migration; raw CREATE UNIQUE INDEX statement

title: A backend field feeding a client-side formatter must be machine-parseable, not pre-formatted
problem: A list view's relative-date formatting silently did nothing — every row showed a raw string instead of 'Today' / 'Yesterday'.
cause: The API returned the date as a pre-formatted display string ('27th Apr 2025'). The client formatter calls Date.parse(), which cannot read that format, fails silently, and falls back to the raw value.
fix: Return the field as an ISO-8601 / RFC3339 timestamp; let the client own all presentation formatting. Removed the now-dead server-side formatting helper.
prevention: Any field that feeds a client-side formatter must be machine-parseable. Keep display formatting on the client — the API sends timestamps, not sentences.

title: Removing a dependency without regenerating the lockfile breaks the frozen-lockfile CI install
problem: A container build failed at the dependency-install step with ERR_PNPM_OUTDATED_LOCKFILE, immediately after a package was removed from package.json. The build never started. Local type-check and lint passed and did not catch it.
cause: CI runs `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`, which refuses to proceed when package.json and the lockfile disagree. Removing a dependency from the manifest without regenerating the lockfile desyncs the two.
fix: Ran `pnpm install --lockfile-only` to regenerate pnpm-lock.yaml, and committed it alongside the manifest change.
prevention: Any dependency add / remove / version change must regenerate and commit the lockfile in the same change. Local type-check and lint never touch the lockfile — only the build does — so verify before pushing.
